items.body_markdown: I think that the main goal of an interview question like this is to see if someone is capable of solving not just a complex problem, but also that they'll solve problems even if they're not that interesting.  I wouldn't want to hire someone who wouldn't work on a project they weren't interested in.  On the other hand, the problem with a question like this is that it could attract people who would rather spend their time solving puzzles instead of just getting the job done. MUST BE CUT IN TESTS

items.body_markdown: Continuing my previous comment, I've hired many people who didn't solve the problems, and rejected many who did. A single question is never the basis for such a decision.
items.body: Continuing my previous comment, I've hired many people who didn't solve the problems, and rejected many who did. A single question is never the basis for such a decision.

items.body_markdown: As someone who has interviewed many times and considered the "add a constraint" questions, there is definitely value to these questions. (a) You want to see how the interviewee deals with changes to the spec (b) see how the interviewee tries to find different approaches (c) weed out people who don't have any clue at all. Basically, the point in these questions is not the final answer, but the thought process. Another advantage of such simple questions is that they can weed out people who knew the question and said they'd never heard it before. I don't like to hire people who lie to me.
items.body: As someone who has interviewed many times and considered the "add a constraint" questions, there is definitely value to these questions. (a) You want to see how the interviewee deals with changes to the spec (b) see how the interviewee tries to find different approaches (c) weed out people who don't have any clue at all. Basically, the point in these questions is not the final answer, but the thought process. Another advantage of such simple questions is that they can weed out people who knew the question and said they'd never heard it before. I don't like to hire people who lie to me.

items.body_markdown: Personally, I think there were several answers that had clever, interesting solutions. I also think that while this could easily be an awful interview question, there could be good value in it, as long as the interviewer is really looking not so much for a completely well-formed solution as looking for whether the interviewee considered approaches that indicate a knowledge of TMP or using constructs in unusual ways. I think it would be bad if this were used this as a pure 'got-it-right/wrong' question, but if it were used as the starting point of a discussion, I could see a lot of value.
items.body: Personally, I think there were several answers that had clever, interesting solutions. I also think that while this could easily be an awful interview question, there could be good value in it, as long as the interviewer is really looking not so much for a completely well-formed solution as looking for whether the interviewee considered approaches that indicate a knowledge of TMP or using constructs in unusual ways. I think it would be bad if this were used this as a pure 'got-it-right/wrong' question, but if it were used as the starting point of a discussion, I could see a lot of value.

items.body_markdown: From my point of view *"Solve this puzzle with one more constraint than the last time it was posted"* question are unwanted, because they admit *"Solve the same puzzle with still another constraint"* and so on *ad nauseum*. To be sure the constructor solution is neat and understanding it means you know something about OO languages, but pone question on the matter is enough.
items.body: From my point of view "Solve this puzzle with one more constraint than the last time it was posted" question are unwanted, because they admit "Solve the same puzzle with still another constraint" and so on ad nauseum. To be sure the constructor solution is neat and understanding it means you know something about OO languages, but pone question on the matter is enough.

items.body_markdown: If instead of C/C++ you were asked to do this in assembly language, with equivalent constraints, it would be impossible. So why are you not allowed to do things that your compiler will make your code do anyways?

items.body_markdown: The interview your chance to shine. Tell them "Without loops or conditionals? Child's play. I can do it without a computer!" Then pull out pen and notepad. They may give you a confused look, but just explain that if you can't count on built in language constructs, you really can't assume anything.

They may give you a confused look, but just explain that if you can't count on built in language constructs, you really can't assume anything.

items.body_markdown: I could understand no loops but why on earth no conditionals!? Theres no true way to do this without conditionals (unless its 1 to infinity) but i know it can be done without loops -edit- maybe templates as its compile time. Also i dont consider function calls as a loop (even if its recursion. Its just a jump/functional call).
